label: Phase 2 — Red Flags & Safety + Core Chronic Disease Management: Lesson Outlines
generationRule: Phase 2 lessons cover (A) RED FLAGS & SAFETY: recognition, risk stratification, and referral/disposition — NO prescribing protocols; and (B) CORE CHRONIC DISEASE MANAGEMENT: guideline-based drug selection, titration thresholds, monitoring, and follow-up. Scope fences SF-01 through SF-06 are fully active. NP-level hard/expert content only. Recognition vs management separation is preserved at the lessonPart boundary within multi-part topics.

lessons.title: Red Flags in Hypertension: Urgency vs Emergency vs Secondary HTN
learningObjectives: Distinguish hypertensive urgency (BP ≥180/120 without end-organ damage) from hypertensive emergency (BP ≥180/120 WITH end-organ damage)
Identify end-organ damage features that mandate ED transfer: encephalopathy, papilledema, AKI, NSTEMI, aortic dissection, pulmonary edema

Recognize secondary hypertension red flags: resistant HTN in young patient, hypokalemia with HTN (hyperaldosteronism), paroxysmal BP spikes (pheochromocytoma), abrupt worsening in older adult (renal artery stenosis)
Apply ACC/AHA BP classification and determine the appropriate clinical response by stage
Determine when ambulatory blood pressure monitoring (ABPM) is indicated versus routine BP follow-up

sections.sectionTitle: Hypertensive Urgency vs Emergency: The Critical Distinction
keyPoints: Urgency: BP ≥180/120 mmHg without signs or symptoms of acute end-organ damage — patient often asymptomatic or has non-specific headache
Emergency: BP ≥180/120 mmHg WITH evidence of acute end-organ damage — neurological, cardiac, renal, ophthalmic, or vascular
Urgency: appropriate to manage outpatient with oral medication adjustment and 24-hour recheck — do NOT send to ED reflexively for high number alone
Emergency: call 911, IV antihypertensive (nitroprusside, nicardipine, labetalol IV) — oral therapy is inadequate for emergency
Neurological: hypertensive encephalopathy (altered mental status, severe headache, visual changes, seizure), posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES), ischemic stroke, hemorrhagic stroke
Cardiac: NSTEMI (chest pain + troponin elevation), acute HF (pulmonary edema, orthopnea, elevated BNP), aortic dissection (sudden tearing chest/back pain + unequal BP in arms)
Renal: rapidly rising creatinine, proteinuria, hematuria — hypertensive nephrosclerosis acutely accelerated
Ophthalmic: papilledema (blurred disc margins), flame hemorrhages, cotton-wool spots — fundoscopic exam should be part of hypertensive emergency assessment
Eclampsia/pre-eclampsia: BP ≥160/110 in pregnant patient + proteinuria + headache + visual changes → obstetric emergency
Primary aldosteronism: resistant HTN + spontaneous hypokalemia (K⁺ <3.5 without diuretic) + adrenal mass; screen with aldosterone-to-renin ratio (ARR >20:1 is suspicious)
Pheochromocytoma: paroxysmal headache-palpitations-diaphoresis triad; episodic severe hypertension; screen with plasma free metanephrines or 24-hour urine catecholamines
Renal artery stenosis: new severe HTN in older adult (atherosclerotic) or young woman (fibromuscular dysplasia), abdominal bruit, AKI after starting ACEi/ARB, asymmetric kidney size on imaging
Cushing syndrome: proximal muscle weakness + moon facies + central obesity + purple striae + hypokalemia + HTN; screen with 24-hour urine free cortisol or 1-mg overnight DST
Obstructive sleep apnea: resistant HTN + obesity + loud snoring + witnessed apneas + morning headache — CPAP compliance often improves BP
White coat HTN: elevated in-office BP with normal out-of-office readings — ABPM diagnostic; lower ASCVD risk than sustained HTN but not entirely benign
Masked HTN: normal in-office BP with elevated out-of-office readings — ABPM essential; ASCVD risk approaches that of sustained HTN
ABPM indications: suspected white coat, masked HTN, borderline Stage 1, resistant HTN evaluation, autonomic dysfunction, nocturnal dipping assessment

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: SF-05 slugA enforced: questions may test recognition of urgency vs emergency, end-organ damage identification, secondary HTN red flags, and ABPM indication. Questions may NOT test drug class selection, step therapy, or antihypertensive prescribing. Those belong to hypertension-step-therapy-guideline (SF-05 slugB).

lessons.title: Neurological Red Flags: Stroke, TIA, and Spinal Emergencies
learningObjectives: Apply the FAST-BE mnemonic to recognize stroke and TIA requiring emergent evaluation
Distinguish ischemic stroke from hemorrhagic stroke and TIA by clinical features and imaging strategy
Identify spinal cord compression red flags (cauda equina syndrome) requiring emergent MRI and surgical consultation
Recognize posterior circulation stroke features that are frequently missed in primary care
Determine appropriate 911 activation criteria for neurological emergencies from the office setting

keyPoints: FAST-BE: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call 911, Balance problems, Eye problems (sudden monocular vision loss, diplopia, hemianopia)

TIA: same focal neurological deficit as stroke but resolves completely within 24 hours (usually minutes); ABCD2 score for 48-hour stroke risk: Age ≥60, BP ≥140/90, Clinical (1 = speech impairment, 2 = unilateral weakness), Duration, Diabetes
TIA with ABCD2 ≥4 or any dual antiplatelet opportunity: urgent neurology same-day or ED evaluation — do NOT discharge and schedule routine follow-up
Ischemic vs hemorrhagic: CT noncontrast head first (rules out hemorrhage); ischemic stroke = thrombolysis decision (tPA if within 4.5h, NIHSS eligible, no contraindications) — requires ED
Posterior circulation stroke: vertigo + ataxia + diplopia + dysphagia + Horner's (PICA infarct = Wallenberg syndrome); often misdiagnosed as 'labyrinthitis' — missing a posterior stroke

sections.sectionTitle: Cauda Equina Syndrome and Spinal Cord Compression
keyPoints: Cauda equina syndrome (CES): low back pain + bilateral leg weakness + saddle anesthesia (perianal/perineal) + bowel/bladder dysfunction (urinary retention > incontinence initially)
CES is a surgical emergency: MRI lumbar spine immediately — delay causes irreversible neurological deficit

Spinal cord compression (from metastasis, abscess, hematoma): progressive bilateral leg weakness + sensory level + bowel/bladder dysfunction; history of cancer or fever + IV drug use (epidural abscess)
Red flag back pain requiring urgent MRI: new bowel/bladder change, saddle anesthesia, bilateral leg weakness, steroid use + fever (epidural abscess), cancer history (metastatic disease)

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: 2A covers stroke/TIA and spinal cord emergencies — recognition and emergent disposition only. Stroke management (tPA eligibility, dual antiplatelet selection) belongs to Phase 3-4. Questions must anchor on RECOGNITION and WHEN TO CALL 911.

lessons.title: Neurological Red Flags: First Seizure, CNS Infection, and Raised ICP
learningObjectives: Identify first-seizure red flags requiring urgent neurological evaluation versus low-risk provoked seizures
Recognize features of CNS infection (bacterial meningitis, viral encephalitis) requiring immediate action
Apply raised ICP recognition criteria and pre-lumbar puncture imaging decision rules
Distinguish new-onset altered mental status causes that require emergent evaluation versus observation
Recognize Guillain-Barré syndrome early features that mandate hospital admission

keyPoints: Provoked seizure (hypoglycemia, hyponatremia, alcohol withdrawal, medication) vs unprovoked first seizure: provoked requires treatment of cause, not necessarily AED
First unprovoked seizure: EEG + brain MRI + metabolic workup; refer neurology for AED decision — NP decision is workup + referral, not starting AED

High-risk first seizure features (admit): prolonged post-ictal state (>30 min), status epilepticus (seizure >5 min or two without full recovery), focal onset, abnormal neuro exam, new structural lesion
Status epilepticus: call 911 + benzo (diazepam, lorazepam) as first-line in office if available; do not delay for IV access

Bacterial meningitis: fever + headache + neck stiffness (classic triad in only 44%) — treat first, image later if herniation risk; LP: CSF WBC >1000 (PMN predominant), elevated protein, low glucose, positive Gram stain/culture
Viral encephalitis (HSV): fever + behavioral change + seizures + temporal lobe focus; MRI temporal lobe abnormality; start IV acyclovir empirically before confirmation
Do NOT delay antibiotics for LP: petechial rash + fever + meningismus = meningococcemia — ceftriaxone 2g IV immediately, then transfer
Raised ICP before LP: papilledema, focal deficit, altered consciousness, new mass lesion → CT head before LP to rule out herniation risk
GBS: ascending bilateral flaccid weakness + areflexia + preceding GI or respiratory infection (Campylobacter most common)
Respiratory compromise risk: FVC <20 mL/kg, NIF <−30 cmH₂O, rapid progression = ICU admission
Autonomic instability: BP lability, cardiac arrhythmias — continuous monitoring required
NP role: recognize ascending weakness pattern + areflexia + recent infection → immediate ED transfer, do NOT manage outpatient

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: 2B focuses on seizure workup triggers, CNS infection recognition, and GBS. No antibiotic dosing or AED selection — those belong to prescribing domain. Stem anchor must differ from Phase 1 headache-differential-red-flags questions (which covered meningitis, SAH, and GCA).

lessons.title: Cancer Red Flags: GI, Pulmonary, and Hematological Malignancy
learningObjectives: Apply NICE 2-week rule criteria for expedited cancer workup referral
Identify colorectal cancer red flags warranting immediate colonoscopy referral
Recognize lung cancer presentation patterns that require urgent CT chest
Distinguish lymphoma B-symptoms and leukemia presentation features from benign causes
Apply iron-deficiency anemia in an older male as a colorectal cancer red flag until proven otherwise

keyPoints: Colorectal cancer: unexplained rectal bleeding in adult >40 (especially right-sided colon → frank occult blood vs left-sided → frank blood), change in bowel habit >4 weeks, unexplained iron-deficiency anemia in male or postmenopausal female (occult GI blood loss), palpable abdominal mass
Gastric cancer: persistent dyspepsia with alarm features in adult >55: weight loss, dysphagia, vomiting, epigastric mass, progressive despite PPI → urgent upper endoscopy
Esophageal cancer: progressive dysphagia (solids before liquids), odynophagia, weight loss — Barrett's esophagus is a precursor in GERD patients
Pancreatic cancer: painless jaundice + weight loss + new-onset DM in adult >50; Courvoisier sign (palpable non-tender gallbladder) — CT pancreas protocol
Lung cancer: smoker (≥40 pack-year) with new cough, hemoptysis, weight loss, or CXR mass → low-dose CT chest screening + urgent referral if symptomatic
Pancoast tumor: shoulder pain + Horner syndrome (ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis) + arm weakness from superior sulcus
Superior vena cava (SVC) syndrome: facial/neck/arm swelling + distended neck veins + headache worse when leaning forward — mediastinal mass (lung cancer or lymphoma) — emergent oncology
Lymphoma: B symptoms (fever >38°C + drenching night sweats + ≥10% weight loss in 6 months) + non-tender rubbery lymphadenopathy + mediastinal mass on CXR → urgent CT staging + hematology referral
Leukemia: fatigue + pallor + petechiae/bruising + recurrent infections + bone pain; CBC: elevated WBC (CML/CLL) or pancytopenia with blasts (AML) → urgent hematology

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: 2A covers GI, pulmonary, and hematological cancer red flags — recognition and referral urgency. Questions must specify the alarm feature in the stem. No overlap with lymphadenopathy Phase 1 questions (np-p1-119–125) — those tested reactive vs malignant node features; 2A tests the ALARM SYMPTOM prompting the workup decision.

lessons.title: Cancer Red Flags: Genitourinary, Breast, Neurological, and Bone
learningObjectives: Identify prostate, bladder, testicular, and cervical cancer red flags requiring urgent referral
Recognize breast cancer presentation features beyond the palpable mass
Apply bone metastasis pain pattern recognition and differentiate from benign musculoskeletal pain
Identify spinal cord compression from cancer metastasis as a neurological emergency
Distinguish malignant from benign causes of weight loss in primary care patients

sections.sectionTitle: GU and Breast Cancer Red Flags
keyPoints: Bladder cancer: painless gross hematuria in adult (most common presentation) → cystoscopy; risk factors: smoking, aniline dye exposure, schistosomiasis
Prostate cancer: elevated PSA trend + lower urinary tract symptoms + bone pain (lytic/blastic lesions in axial skeleton) — but PSA alone is not diagnostic; digital rectal exam + urology referral
Testicular cancer: painless testicular mass in young male (15–35) — assume malignant until proven otherwise; scrotal ultrasound, AFP, beta-hCG, LDH tumor markers

Breast cancer beyond the mass: nipple discharge (bloody, unilateral), nipple inversion (new onset), skin changes (peau d'orange = inflammatory breast cancer — red, swollen breast without fever), axillary lymphadenopathy without breast mass

sections.sectionTitle: Bone Metastasis and Spinal Cord Compression
keyPoints: Bone metastasis pain: constant, progressive, worse at night, not relieved by rest (vs mechanical pain which is activity-related); axial skeleton predominance (spine, pelvis, proximal femur, skull)
Common primary cancers that metastasize to bone: breast, prostate, lung, kidney, thyroid (mnemonic: 'Breast Promenades Long Kidney Trails')
Pathological fracture risk: lytic lesion >2.5 cm in femur or >50% cortical destruction → orthopedic surgery referral to prevent fracture
Malignant spinal cord compression: progressive back pain + bilateral leg weakness + bladder/bowel change in patient with known cancer → MRI spine emergently + dexamethasone + radiation oncology
Definition: ≥5% body weight loss in 6–12 months without intentional effort
Cancer workup threshold: age >50, weight loss >10% body weight, or associated alarm symptoms (dysphagia, rectal bleeding, hemoptysis, lymphadenopathy)
Initial screen: CBC, CMP, TSH, UA, CXR, age/sex-appropriate cancer screening — targeted by alarm symptom cluster
If initial screen negative in high-risk patient: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis; PET scan for occult malignancy

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: 2B focuses on GU, breast, bone, and systemic cancer red flags. No treatment content — referral urgency and workup are the terminal clinical actions tested.

lessons.title: Pediatric Red Flags: Neonatal and Infant Don't-Miss Diagnoses
learningObjectives: Identify neonatal red flags requiring immediate transfer: bilious vomiting, cyanosis with feeding, temperature instability, petechiae
Recognize febrile infant (<90 days) risk stratification and criteria for septic workup vs outpatient management
Apply the Pediatric Assessment Triangle (PAT) for rapid triage of the ill-appearing infant
Identify congenital heart disease presentations that are missed after NICU discharge
Recognize non-accidental trauma (NAT) pattern injuries and mandatory reporting obligation

keyPoints: Bilious (green) vomiting in any neonate = malrotation with midgut volvulus until proven otherwise → emergent surgical evaluation; do NOT wait for contrast study before calling surgery

Cyanosis with feeding that improves with crying: ductus-dependent congenital heart lesion (e.g., critical pulmonic stenosis, transposition of great arteries) — hyperoxia test (FiO2 1.0 → if PaO2 <150, cardiac shunt suspected)
Hyperbilirubinemia in first 24 hours: pathological (hemolytic disease) — needs immediate phototherapy and workup
Temperature instability in newborn: sepsis until proven otherwise — group B Strep, Listeria, HSV in first weeks of life
<28 days with any fever ≥38°C: full septic workup (CBC, CMP, UA/culture, blood culture, CSF, RSV/flu if applicable) + admission + empiric antibiotics — no exceptions
29–60 days with fever: step-down protocol (Philadelphia, Boston, Rochester criteria); if all low-risk criteria met and reliable follow-up → outpatient with 24h recheck; otherwise admit
61–90 days: well-appearing without source and reassuring labs → outpatient is acceptable for some; must have reliable caregiver and follow-up within 24 hours
Ill-appearing at any age with fever = admit regardless of lab values

NAT pattern injuries: (1) posterior rib fractures in infants (pathognomonic — from squeezing), (2) metaphyseal 'bucket-handle' or 'corner' fractures, (3) retinal hemorrhages (shaken baby syndrome), (4) bruising in non-mobile infants (any bruise in infant not yet cruising is suspicious)
Classic mismatch: severity of injury inconsistent with developmental age or stated mechanism
Mandatory reporting: NP has a legal obligation to report suspected NAT to child protective services — report on suspicion, not confirmed diagnosis
Workup: full skeletal survey, retinal exam, head CT or MRI (non-contrast), labs to exclude coagulopathy

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: 2A covers neonatal/infant red flags. Must not duplicate abdominal pain Phase 1 topics (which covered ectopic, intussusception, volvulus at the diagnostic level — 2A tests the critical action trigger: when to call 911 or transfer vs the differential reasoning). Specify infant age clearly in all stems.

lessons.title: Pediatric Red Flags: Toddler, School Age, and Adolescent Emergencies
learningObjectives: Recognize Kawasaki disease and its diagnostic criteria and cardiac complication risk
Identify meningococcal disease and other pediatric infectious emergencies requiring 911 activation
Apply PECARN pediatric head CT decision rules for low-risk versus high-risk head trauma
Recognize epiglottitis, croup, and foreign body aspiration by clinical features and appropriate disposition
Identify acute rheumatic fever Jones criteria and the consequence of missed streptococcal pharyngitis

keyPoints: Classic Kawasaki: fever ≥5 days + 4 of 5 CRASH criteria: Conjunctivitis (bilateral non-purulent), Rash (polymorphous), Adenopathy (cervical, unilateral >1.5 cm), Strawberry tongue/lip cracking/oral mucosal changes, Hand/foot changes (erythema → periungual desquamation in convalescent phase)
Incomplete Kawasaki: fever ≥5 days + fewer than 4 criteria but elevated CRP/ESR + echo changes — IVIG decision requires pediatric cardiology
Cardiac complication: coronary artery aneurysm (CAA) — the reason treatment matters; echocardiogram at diagnosis
Treatment decision (IVIG + aspirin) belongs to pediatrics/cardiology; NP role is early recognition and pediatric referral

Epiglottitis: drooling + dysphagia + muffled 'hot potato' voice + high fever + tripod positioning; do NOT examine throat (laryngospasm risk) — immediate ENT/anesthesia + ICU; 'thumbprint sign' on lateral neck XR

Croup: viral (parainfluenza), barky seal-like cough, inspiratory stridor, nocturnal, typically <3 years, low-grade fever; Steeple sign on AP neck XR; mild = humidified air + dexamethasone; moderate/severe = racemic epinephrine
Foreign body aspiration: toddler with sudden cough + wheeze + air-trapping (unilateral hyperinflation on expiratory CXR) → bronchoscopy; do NOT perform abdominal thrusts if still breathing

sections.sectionTitle: Acute Rheumatic Fever and Pediatric Cardiac Red Flags
keyPoints: ARF Jones criteria (major): carditis, polyarthritis (migratory), Sydenham's chorea, erythema marginatum, subcutaneous nodules — 2 major OR 1 major + 2 minor (elevated ESR/CRP, prolonged PR, fever, elevated WBC) + evidence of recent GAS infection
Consequence: mitral stenosis from recurrent ARF — prevent with penicillin prophylaxis (secondary prevention)
New murmur in child with recent pharyngitis + fever + migratory arthritis → ECG + echo + cardiology
PECARN head CT rule: low-risk if: GCS 15, no LOC (or brief <5 sec), no vomiting, no severe headache, no scalp hematoma (temporal/parietal/occipital), normal behavior → observation instead of CT

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: 2B focuses on older pediatric emergencies. Kawasaki questions must include the fever duration (≥5 days) as a key stem element. Epiglottitis vs croup must be distinguishable by age, fever severity, and drooling. No overlap with Phase 1 fever-without-source topics (which covered febrile infant workup at the differential level — 2B tests the red flag action).

lessons.title: Psychiatric Red Flags: Suicidality and Lethal Self-Harm Risk
lessons.scopeFenceNote: SF-04 slugA boundary: MDD diagnosis and suicidality risk stratification are already scoped to mdd-diagnosis-risk-initial-management. 2A extends that into the HIGH-RISK action domain — what the NP does when risk is established, not how to diagnose or select antidepressants.
learningObjectives: Apply the Columbia Suicide Severity Rating Scale (C-SSRS) to stratify suicidal ideation from passive to active with plan and means
Differentiate low, moderate, and high suicide risk and the corresponding NP clinical actions
Recognize protective and exacerbating factors that modify suicide risk estimate
Implement a safety plan and determine when involuntary psychiatric hold (5150/Baker Act) is legally indicated
Identify patient populations with disproportionately elevated suicide risk: older white males, LGBTQ+ youth, Native American communities

keyPoints: Passive ideation only ('I wish I were dead') + no history of attempts + strong protective factors → outpatient safety plan + increased follow-up frequency
Active ideation WITHOUT plan or intent → assess intent and plan; consider same-day psychiatry consult vs outpatient safety plan with follow-up within 48–72 hours
Active ideation WITH plan AND available means → involuntary hold (5150/Baker Act) or direct to ED with safety companion; do NOT allow patient to leave unescorted
Recent attempt (within 90 days) + current ideation → psychiatric inpatient evaluation required regardless of stated intent
Protective: religious objection to suicide, strong social support, responsibility for children, reasons for living, treatment engagement, no substance use, no prior attempts

High-risk markers: prior attempt (strongest predictor), male sex, white or Native American race, age >65 or adolescent male, LGBTQ+ identity without affirmative support, recent loss (job, relationship, legal problem), access to firearms
Means restriction counseling: if firearms in home → ask patient AND family/partner to store separately or remove from access; reduces suicide completion by 30–90%
Medications lethality: tricyclics, lithium, opioids — only prescribe small quantities in suicidal patients; consider guardian-controlled dispensing
Safety plan components (Stanley-Brown model): warning signs → coping strategies → social contacts → professional contacts → means restriction → emergency services
Duty to warn (Tarasoff): if patient expresses specific threat to identifiable third party → NP must notify potential victim AND law enforcement; document
Involuntary hold criteria: danger to self OR others OR gravely disabled (unable to provide food/shelter/clothing due to mental disorder)
Documentation: document C-SSRS score, clinical reasoning, patient response to safety plan, and plan for follow-up — medicolegally critical

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: SF-04 boundary respected: 2A tests what to DO when suicide risk is HIGH — not how to diagnose MDD or which antidepressant to select. No antidepressant selection questions appear here. Do not duplicate MDD recognition questions from Phase 1 (np-p1-056).

lessons.title: Psychiatric Red Flags: New-Onset Psychosis and Acute Agitation
learningObjectives: Generate a medical differential for new-onset psychosis to exclude organic causes before psychiatric diagnosis
Recognize first-episode psychosis features requiring emergent psychiatric evaluation
Apply delirium vs dementia vs psychosis clinical distinctions
Identify violence risk markers and safe management of the acutely agitated patient in primary care
Determine when involuntary psychiatric hospitalization is indicated for psychosis

keyPoints: Must-exclude organic causes of acute psychosis: hypoglycemia, hyponatremia, hypoxia, sepsis (especially in elderly), thyroid storm, Addisonian crisis, hepatic encephalopathy, B12 deficiency, syphilis, HIV encephalitis, CNS tumors or vasculitis, steroid psychosis, anticholinergic toxidrome
First-episode psychosis workup: CBC, CMP, TSH, B12, RPR/VDRL, HIV, UA, urine drug screen, LFTs; brain MRI if focal findings or concern for structural cause

Delirium vs psychosis: delirium = acute fluctuating consciousness + inattention + disorganized thinking + usually identifiable medical cause; psychosis = clear consciousness + fixed delusions/hallucinations without clouding
Wernicke encephalopathy: classic triad (confusion + ophthalmoplegia + ataxia) in alcoholic or malnourished patient — thiamine 500 mg IV before glucose

sections.sectionTitle: Acute Agitation Safety in Primary Care
keyPoints: De-escalation first: low stimulus environment, calm voice, non-threatening posture, offer options not ultimatums — most effective and least restrictive
If de-escalation fails and patient is danger to self/others: call 911 for restraint assistance; do NOT attempt physical restraint without security/law enforcement
Excited delirium syndrome: extreme agitation + hyperthermia + superhuman strength + tachycardia + diaphoresis — in-custody risk of sudden death; emergent medical care required
Involuntary hold: if patient is gravely disabled or danger to self/others due to psychiatric condition → initiate involuntary hold procedures per state law; clearly document clinical basis

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: 2B covers first-episode psychosis organic workup and acute agitation — no antipsychotic drug selection (that belongs to prescribing domain Phase 3). Delirium vs psychosis distinction must not replicate altered mental status content from headache (ICP) or syncope Phase 1 questions.

lessons.title: Heart Failure Phenotype Classification and Monitoring
learningObjectives: Classify heart failure by ejection fraction: HFrEF (EF <40%), HFmrEF (EF 41–49%), HFpEF (EF ≥50%)
Apply NYHA functional classification to guide treatment intensity and prognosis communication
Interpret BNP and NT-proBNP for HF diagnosis, monitoring treatment response, and identifying decompensation
Distinguish HFrEF from HFpEF by clinical features and echocardiographic findings
Identify triggers for acute decompensated HF and appropriate NP response

keyPoints: HFrEF (reduced EF <40%): systolic dysfunction; dilated cardiomyopathy pattern; responds to GDMT with mortality benefit (ACEi/ARNi, BB, MRA, SGLT-2)

HFpEF (preserved EF ≥50%): diastolic dysfunction; typical patient is elderly, hypertensive, female, obese with DM; preserved heart size but elevated filling pressures; fewer mortality-reducing therapies
HFmrEF (mildly reduced EF 41–49%): intermediate; similar GDMT benefit as HFrEF for some agents

Key clinical features distinguishing HFrEF from HFpEF: HFrEF = S3 gallop + displaced PMI + dilated cardiomegaly; HFpEF = no S3 + preserved heart size + diastolic parameters on echo (elevated E/e' ratio, impaired relaxation)
Diagnostic: BNP >400 pg/mL strongly suggests HF; BNP <100 pg/mL argues against HF in acute dyspnea; NT-proBNP thresholds are 3–4× higher than BNP
Monitoring: rising BNP on stable GDMT = suboptimal decongestion or worsening; falling BNP = favorable response to therapy
BNP is elevated in obesity (misleadingly low false negative), renal failure (falsely elevated), and AF (moderately elevated baseline)
At HF clinic visit: check BNP + weight + fluid status (JVD, edema, rales) to guide diuretic adjustment — not just symptoms
NYHA I: no symptoms; NYHA II: symptoms with moderate exertion; NYHA III: symptoms with minimal exertion; NYHA IV: symptoms at rest

Decompensation triggers: dietary sodium excess (most common), medication non-adherence (diuretics, ACEi, BB), new AF with rapid ventricular rate, myocardial ischemia, infection, NSAID use, addition of negative inotrope

Decompensation recognition: weight gain >2 lb in 24h or >5 lb in 1 week, orthopnea, PND, increasing edema, worsening dyspnea → action: increase diuretic dose AND schedule urgent follow-up within 48–72 hours or direct to ED if unstable

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: SF-06 2A boundary: this lesson owns EF classification, BNP interpretation, NYHA staging, phenotype recognition, and decompensation trigger identification. It may NOT include GDMT drug selection, ARNi advantages over ACEi, beta-blocker class preference, or SGLT-2 indication for HF — those belong to lesson 2B.

lessons.title: Heart Failure GDMT: Four-Pillar Pharmacotherapy and Monitoring
learningObjectives: Apply the four-pillar GDMT framework for HFrEF: ACEi/ARNi, evidence-based beta-blocker, MRA, SGLT-2 inhibitor
Distinguish sacubitril/valsartan (ARNi) from ACE inhibitors: mechanism, PARADIGM-HF trial superiority, and mandatory 36-hour washout
Select evidence-based beta-blockers for HFrEF and exclude non-approved agents
Identify MRA indications, monitoring parameters (potassium, creatinine), and contraindications
Apply DAPA-HF/EMPEROR-Reduced data: SGLT-2 inhibitor benefit for HFrEF independent of diabetes status

keyPoints: Pillar 1 — ACEi or ARNi: initiate ACEi (lisinopril) if tolerated; upgrade to ARNi (sacubitril/valsartan) for ambulatory HFrEF patients who can tolerate — 36-hour ACEi washout before starting ARNi to prevent angioedema risk; ARNi cannot be combined with ACEi

Pillar 2 — Evidence-based BB: ONLY carvedilol (non-selective alpha/BB), metoprolol succinate (selective BB), or bisoprolol are HF-approved; do NOT use atenolol, propranolol, or metoprolol tartrate — titrate slowly starting at low dose; avoid initiation during decompensation

Pillar 3 — MRA (spironolactone or eplerenone): for EF ≤35% + NYHA Class II–IV despite ACEi/BB; monitor K⁺ and Cr at 1 week + 4 weeks; hold if K⁺ >5.0 or eGFR <30; eplerenone preferred in males (less gynecomastia)

Pillar 4 — SGLT-2 inhibitor (dapagliflozin or empagliflozin): proven HF mortality benefit in HFrEF regardless of T2DM status; benefits driven by hemodynamic/decongestion mechanisms, not glucose lowering
Mechanism: neprilysin inhibitor (sacubitril) + ARB (valsartan) — increases natriuretic peptides + blocks RAAS
PARADIGM-HF trial: ARNi reduced CV death or HF hospitalization by 20% vs enalapril in EF ≤40%; NNT ≈16 over 27 months
Starting conditions: stable outpatient HFrEF on ACEi for ≥4 weeks (or ARB), SBP ≥100, eGFR ≥30, K⁺ ≤5.0
Critical safety: 36-hour washout after LAST dose of ACEi before FIRST dose of ARNi; otherwise risk of angioedema — this is a frequently tested NP-level prescribing decision
No proven mortality-reducing GDMT for HFpEF (unlike HFrEF)
EMPEROR-Preserved trial: empagliflozin reduced HF hospitalization in HFpEF — now recommended
Control comorbidities driving HFpEF: HTN (BP target <130/80), AF rate control, DM optimization, obesity/sleep apnea treatment
Diuretics: loop diuretics (furosemide, torsemide) for congestion management; electrolyte and renal monitoring required

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: SF-06 2B boundary: This lesson owns GDMT drug selection and initiation decisions. Cross-topic fence: ace-arb-arni-indications-monitoring owns drug-specific AE management, pharmacokinetics, and monitoring schedules per individual agent. Questions in 2B test WHICH class to add and WHEN — not specific AE management for an individual drug.

lessons.title: Hypertension: First-Line Drug Selection and Compelling Indications
learningObjectives: Apply compelling indication framework to select antihypertensive class by comorbidity
Distinguish first-line agents by race, age, and end-organ comorbidity per JNC/AHA 2017 guidelines
Identify contraindicated antihypertensive agents by comorbidity (ACEi/ARB in pregnancy, beta-blockers in COPD with bronchospasm)
Select optimal initial therapy for isolated systolic hypertension in the elderly
Apply the principle of combination therapy to maximize BP reduction with minimal adverse effects

keyPoints: DM or CKD with proteinuria: ACEi or ARB first-line (renoprotective via RAAS blockade) — preferred regardless of race
HFrEF: ACEi/ARNi + evidence-based BB + MRA — thiazides and CCBs are not first-line for HF
Post-MI/stable angina: beta-blocker (cardioprotective) + ACEi/ARB
Black patients without DM/CKD: thiazide diuretic (chlorthalidone preferred over HCTZ) or CCB (amlodipine) — ACEi/ARB LESS effective as monotherapy in Black patients (lower renin activity on average)
Isolated systolic HTN in elderly (ALLHAT trial): thiazide and CCB equally effective; avoid beta-blocker as first-line in elderly (less BP lowering benefit, fall risk from bradycardia)
Thiazide diuretics: chlorthalidone (preferred over HCTZ — longer acting, lower CV risk in ALLHAT); adverse effects: hyponatremia, hypokalemia, hyperuricemia (gout caution), hyperglycemia

ACE inhibitors (lisinopril, enalapril, ramipril): first-line with DM/CKD/HF; adverse effect: dry cough (10–15% — switch to ARB); angioedema (rare but life-threatening — absolute switch to ARB, NOT re-challenge)
ARBs (losartan, valsartan, olmesartan): same indication as ACEi without cough; both ACEi and ARB are CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy (teratogenic) and should not be combined

Calcium channel blockers (amlodipine): effective in Black patients and elderly; adverse effect: peripheral edema (dose-related), constipation, gingival hyperplasia (with diltiazem/verapamil); non-dihydropyridines (diltiazem, verapamil) — avoid in HFrEF (negative inotropes)
Beta-blockers: reserved for compelling indications (post-MI, HF, angina, rate control in AF) — not first-line for uncomplicated HTN per JNC guidelines
Preferred combination: ACEi (or ARB) + CCB (amlodipine) — synergistic, less AE overlap; CCB edema mitigated by ACEi/ARB (vasodilation offset effect)
Avoid: ACEi + ARB combined (ONTARGET trial: increased AKI and hyperkalemia without added benefit); avoid ACEi + ARB + aldosterone antagonist triple RAAS block
If 2 drugs insufficient: add chlorthalidone (or HCTZ if not already on diuretic) → 3-drug regimen
Resistant HTN (uncontrolled on ≥3 drugs including diuretic): add spironolactone (PATHWAY-2 trial: most effective add-on); evaluate for secondary HTN

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: SF-05 slugB enforced: questions focus on drug class selection and compelling indications. May NOT test urgency vs emergency distinction, secondary HTN recognition, or BP staging criteria — those belong to hypertension-red-flags-referral (SF-05 slugA). No overlap with cardiovascular Phase 1 diagnostic content.

lessons.title: Hypertension: Step Therapy, Resistant HTN, and Special Populations
learningObjectives: Apply step therapy escalation for uncontrolled hypertension on monotherapy
Identify medication-related causes of resistant hypertension (NSAIDs, decongestants, OCPs, stimulants)
Manage hypertension in chronic kidney disease and diabetes with kidney protection in mind
Apply BP targets by comorbidity: DM, CKD, elderly patients, stroke prevention
Recognize the renal dosing requirement for RAAS inhibitors and monitor for hyperkalemia and AKI

keyPoints: Step 1: 1 drug (ACEi or ARB, CCB, or thiazide — based on compelling indication); titrate to maximum tolerated dose before adding second agent
Step 2: 2-drug combination: preferred = ACEi/ARB + CCB; alternative = ACEi/ARB + thiazide; if CCB + thiazide first, add ACEi/ARB
Step 3: 3-drug combination: ACEi/ARB + CCB + thiazide — most patients will reach goal on 3 drugs
Step 4 (resistant): add spironolactone 25–50 mg daily — greatest evidence for resistant HTN; also bisoprolol, doxazosin, or amiloride as alternatives
NSAIDs: blunt antihypertensive efficacy of ACEi/ARB and diuretics (prostaglandin-mediated sodium retention) — most common cause of medication-induced resistant HTN
Combined oral contraceptives: estrogen component causes sodium retention and RAAS activation; BP should be monitored 3 months after OCP initiation
Stimulants: decongestants (pseudoephedrine), amphetamines, cocaine — sympathomimetic BP elevation
Others: corticosteroids, cyclosporine, tacrolimus, erythropoietin — each mechanistically elevates BP
DM: target <130/80 (ADA 2024); ACEi or ARB first-line regardless of race — reduces microalbuminuria progression
CKD with proteinuria: target <130/80; ACEi or ARB first-line — SGLT-2 inhibitor add-on for proteinuria reduction (CREDENCE/DAPA-CKD)
Elderly (SPRINT-eligible): target <130/80 systolic reduces CV events — but caution with orthostatic hypotension, fall risk; check orthostatics at each visit
Post-stroke/TIA: ACEi or ARB + thiazide combination most evidence-based for secondary prevention; target <130/80

RAAS monitoring after initiation: check BMP (K⁺, creatinine) at 2–4 weeks after starting ACEi/ARB; a rise in creatinine ≤30% from baseline is acceptable and expected; >30% rise → hold and evaluate for bilateral renal artery stenosis

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: 2B extends step therapy and special populations — same slug as 2A. Questions must not duplicate 2A first-line selection stems. Each 2B stem must be anchored to either step escalation, medication-induced HTN, or a specific comorbid population (CKD/DM/elderly).

lessons.title: Atrial Fibrillation: Stroke Risk Stratification and Anticoagulation
learningObjectives: Apply CHA₂DS₂-VASc score to stratify stroke risk and determine anticoagulation indication
Select appropriate DOAC versus warfarin for AF anticoagulation based on patient profile
Apply HAS-BLED score to assess bleeding risk and identify modifiable bleeding risk factors
Recognize AF types: paroxysmal, persistent, long-standing persistent, and permanent
Manage anticoagulation around cardioversion: 3-week before + 4-week after rule or TEE-guided strategy

keyPoints: CHA₂DS₂-VASc: Congestive HF (1), Hypertension (1), Age ≥75 (2), Diabetes (1), Stroke/TIA/thromboembolism prior (2), Vascular disease (1), Age 65–74 (1), Sex category female (1)
Anticoagulation threshold: men ≥2, women ≥3 → anticoagulate; men = 1, women = 2 → consider; men = 0, women = 0–1 → no anticoagulation

DOAC vs warfarin: DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran, edoxaban) preferred over warfarin for non-valvular AF — lower intracranial bleed risk, no INR monitoring, comparable or superior stroke prevention
Warfarin still preferred: mechanical heart valves, moderate-severe mitral stenosis — DOACs are contraindicated in these settings
Apixaban (Eliquis): most commonly prescribed; twice-daily dosing; dose-reduce if ≥2 of: age ≥80, weight ≤60 kg, creatinine ≥1.5 mg/dL; lowest GI bleed risk among DOACs
Rivaroxaban (Xarelto): once-daily with evening meal; avoid if eGFR <15; higher GI bleed risk than apixaban
Dabigatran (Pradaxa): avoid if eGFR <30; highest GI bleed risk; dyspepsia common adverse effect; only DOAC with FDA-approved reversal agent (idarucizumab)
Reversal agents: andexanet alfa (apixaban, rivaroxaban); idarucizumab (dabigatran); 4-factor PCC for all DOACs in life-threatening bleed if specific agent unavailable
AF duration ≥48 hours OR unknown duration before cardioversion: anticoagulate ≥3 weeks before cardioversion AND ≥4 weeks after — risk of thrombus formation during post-cardioversion atrial stunning
TEE-guided strategy: if urgent cardioversion needed → transesophageal echocardiogram to exclude left atrial appendage (LAA) thrombus; if no thrombus → cardiovert then anticoagulate ≥4 weeks
AF duration <48 hours in low-risk patient: cardioversion may proceed without 3-week pre-treatment if anticoagulation started immediately; post-cardioversion anticoagulation still required ≥4 weeks
Do NOT stop long-term anticoagulation after successful cardioversion if CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2 — paroxysmal AF is still present

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: 2A covers stroke risk stratification and anticoagulation selection. Questions must be specific to DOAC selection, CHA₂DS₂-VASc calculation, or cardioversion anticoagulation protocol. No rate/rhythm control drug selection — that belongs to 2B.

lessons.title: Atrial Fibrillation: Rate Control vs Rhythm Control Strategy
learningObjectives: Apply rate control goals and select appropriate rate control agents by HF status
Distinguish rate control from rhythm control and identify when rhythm control is preferred
Select appropriate antiarrhythmic agents for AF rhythm control and their contraindications
Recognize AF with RVR as a clinical emergency requiring immediate rate management
Apply the EAST-AFNET 4 trial evidence for early rhythm control

keyPoints: Rate control goal: resting HR <80 bpm in symptomatic patients; <110 bpm is acceptable in asymptomatic patients with preserved EF (RACE II trial)
Beta-blockers (metoprolol, carvedilol): first-line for rate control; preferred in HFrEF because they improve HF outcomes AND control rate
Non-dihydropyridine CCBs (diltiazem, verapamil): effective rate control; CONTRAINDICATED in HFrEF (negative inotropes — worsen LV function); safe in HFpEF

Digoxin: rate control at rest only (does not control rate with exercise); adjunct therapy; narrow therapeutic window; check levels in elderly and CKD; toxicity: nausea, bradycardia, visual changes, arrhythmia
AF with RVR + hemodynamic instability (hypotension, pulmonary edema, ACS): electrical cardioversion immediately — not pharmacological rate control
EAST-AFNET 4 trial: early rhythm control in AF patients with ≥1 risk factor for stroke reduced CV events vs usual care — rhythm control now preferred early

Antiarrhythmic agents for AF rhythm control: flecainide/propafenone (no structural heart disease only), amiodarone (most effective, safe with HF and LV dysfunction — but extensive SE profile), sotalol (requires QTc monitoring — avoid if QTc >500 ms)
Dofetilide: hospital-only initiation due to QTc prolongation monitoring requirement
Catheter ablation: pulmonary vein isolation — preferred in patients who fail or cannot tolerate antiarrhythmic drugs; increasingly used as first-line rhythm control in young/active patients
Pill-in-pocket approach: flecainide or propafenone taken at onset of AF episode in patients with rare episodes and no structural heart disease

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: 2B covers rate vs rhythm control. No anticoagulation/CHA₂DS₂-VASc content (that is 2A). Questions on amiodarone, flecainide, or digoxin must specify the cardiac structural status in the stem — this is the key clinical differentiator.

lessons.title: T2DM Intensification: Agent Classes, A1c Targets, and Hypoglycemia Risk
learningObjectives: Select A1c targets by patient profile: standard (<7%), relaxed (<8%), very relaxed (<8.5%) — individualized by age, comorbidity, life expectancy, and hypoglycemia risk
Compare second-line agent classes for T2DM: SU, DPP-4i, TZD, GLP-1 RA, SGLT-2i at the map level (which class for which patient type without cardiorenal indications)
Stratify hypoglycemia risk across T2DM medications: sulfonylureas and insulin (high); DPP-4i, GLP-1 RA, SGLT-2i (low independent risk)
Apply insulin initiation decision criteria: when to start basal insulin in T2DM, typical starting dose
Distinguish sulfonylurea comparative selection (glipizide vs glimepiride vs glyburide) by renal safety

sections.sectionTitle: A1c Target Selection by Patient Profile
keyPoints: Standard target <7.0%: most non-pregnant adults with T2DM, low hypoglycemia risk, motivated patient with good self-management
Relaxed target <8.0%: older adults (>70) with moderate hypoglycemia risk, limited life expectancy, established cardiovascular disease, fall risk, cognitive impairment
Very relaxed <8.5%: elderly with multiple comorbidities, frailty, end-organ failure, where tight control risks more than it benefits
Tight vs relaxed: ACCORD trial showed intensive control (<6.0%) increased mortality in high CV-risk T2DM patients — the lesson: individualize, not maximize control

sections.sectionTitle: Agent Class Map for T2DM Intensification
keyPoints: Sulfonylureas (glipizide, glimepiride, glyburide): effective, inexpensive; HIGH hypoglycemia risk; avoid glyburide in elderly or CKD (active metabolite accumulation); glimepiride preferred if CKD eGFR 30–45
DPP-4 inhibitors (sitagliptin, saxagliptin, linagliptin): low hypoglycemia risk, weight neutral, well-tolerated; note: saxagliptin may increase HF hospitalizations (SAVOR-TIMI) — avoid in HF
Thiazolidinediones/TZD (pioglitazone): improve insulin sensitivity; useful in NAFLD; contraindicated in HF (fluid retention); associated with bladder cancer and fracture risk

GLP-1 RA and SGLT-2i: agent-level differences and cardiorenal trial data owned by SF-01 slugB (glp1-sglt2-comorbidity-selection) — in THIS lesson, only map-level use: GLP-1 RA when weight loss is a priority, SGLT-2i when established ASCVD/HF/CKD is present

Indications to start insulin: A1c >10–12% at diagnosis or not meeting target on ≥3 oral agents, symptomatic hyperglycemia (polyuria, weight loss), hospital admission requiring insulin, DKA (then T1DM reassessment needed)
Starting basal insulin: begin with 10 units/day (or 0.1–0.2 units/kg/day) of long-acting insulin (glargine or detemir); titrate by 2 units every 3 days until fasting BG 80–130 mg/dL
Continuing metformin with insulin: recommended — reduces insulin requirement and weight gain; hold if eGFR <30 or contrast exposure
Hypoglycemia teaching at insulin initiation: patient must know glucagon use, recognition of <70 mg/dL symptoms, and 15-15 rule for correction

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: SF-01 slugA enforced: questions may NOT include cardiorenal trial data, GLP-1 mechanism/AE profile, or SGLT-2 mechanism/AE profile. Those belong to glp1-sglt2-comorbidity-selection. No overlap with Phase 1 DKA/HHS recognition questions (np-p1-127–129 covered acute recognition only). Stems must anchor to intensification scenarios (A1c not at goal, need to add agent) — not diagnosis or acute management.

lessons.title: T2DM Follow-Up Management: Monitoring, Complications Screening, and Patient Barriers
learningObjectives: Apply the T2DM monitoring schedule: A1c frequency, fasting lipids, UACR, eGFR, dilated eye exam, foot exam, dental care
Recognize early microvascular complication findings: diabetic nephropathy (microalbuminuria), retinopathy, neuropathy
Identify and address barriers to T2DM medication adherence: cost, hypoglycemia fear, injection hesitancy, health literacy
Adjust T2DM medications at follow-up for eGFR decline: metformin hold, SU renal caution, GLP-1 RA and SGLT-2i CKD thresholds
Apply motivational interviewing principles to improve patient engagement in T2DM self-management

keyPoints: A1c: every 3 months if not at goal or medication change; every 6 months if stable at goal
UACR (urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio): annually; microalbuminuria = 30–299 mg/g (initiate or intensify ACEi/ARB); macroalbuminuria = ≥300 mg/g
eGFR: annually; guides metformin safety (hold if eGFR <30, caution 30–45), SGLT-2i threshold (most require eGFR ≥20–30 for HF indication, ≥30–45 for glycemic indication)
Dilated fundus exam: annually if any retinopathy; every 1–2 years if no retinopathy
Foot exam: at every visit for high-risk feet (neuropathy, PVD, prior ulcer); at least annually for all patients
Fasting lipids: annually; statin therapy in T2DM 40–75 without established ASCVD (moderate-intensity statin)
Diabetic nephropathy: microalbuminuria is the earliest marker (precedes creatinine rise by years); initiate ACEi or ARB even in normotensive patient with microalbuminuria

Diabetic retinopathy: non-proliferative (microaneurysms, hemorrhages, exudates) → proliferative (neovascularization, risk of vitreous hemorrhage and retinal detachment) — refer to ophthalmology at each stage

Diabetic neuropathy: distal symmetric polyneuropathy (DPN): stocking-glove pattern + loss of vibration sense + absent ankle reflexes; screen with 10g monofilament + tuning fork; treat neuropathic pain with duloxetine, pregabalin, or TCAs

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: 2B covers monitoring, complication screening, and follow-up adjustment. Must not include GLP-1/SGLT-2 mechanism or cardiorenal trial data (SF-01 slugA). Stems must be anchored to existing patient on therapy — follow-up visit scenario, not initial prescribing.

lessons.title: Thyroid Disease: Diagnosis, Staging, and Treatment Initiation Decision
learningObjectives: Interpret TSH, free T4, and free T3 to classify thyroid status: primary vs central, overt vs subclinical
Apply treatment initiation thresholds for subclinical hypothyroidism by TSH level and patient profile
Distinguish Graves disease from Hashimoto thyroiditis and thyroid nodule workup overview
Identify referral criteria to endocrinology: refractory thyroid disease, pregnancy, suspected thyroid malignancy
Recognize Graves disease as the most common cause of hyperthyroidism and its clinical features

keyPoints: Primary hypothyroidism: elevated TSH + low free T4 — thyroid gland failure (Hashimoto most common cause in iodine-sufficient areas)

Subclinical hypothyroidism: elevated TSH + NORMAL free T4 — treat if TSH >10 mIU/L (regardless of symptoms); consider treating if TSH 5–10 with symptoms, pregnancy desire, or anti-TPO antibody positive
Central hypothyroidism (secondary/tertiary): low TSH + low free T4 — pituitary or hypothalamic dysfunction; TSH is unreliable, use free T4 to gauge severity
Primary hyperthyroidism: suppressed TSH + elevated free T4 (and/or T3) — most common cause is Graves disease; subclinical hyperthyroidism: suppressed TSH + normal free T4/T3

Graves disease: diffuse goiter + exophthalmos (proptosis) + pretibial myxedema + thyroid bruit; TSI (thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulin) antibodies are pathognomonic; most common cause of hyperthyroidism

Hashimoto thyroiditis: most common cause of hypothyroidism in developed countries; anti-TPO antibody elevated; diffuse firm goiter; progressive lymphocytic destruction; may have transient hyperthyroid phase (Hashitoxicosis)

Thyroid nodule workup: palpable or incidental; TSH first — if suppressed → hyperfunctioning nodule (benign); if normal/elevated → ultrasound for characterization → FNAB if suspicious features (hypoechogenic, irregular margins, microcalcifications, >1 cm with high-risk features)
Malignancy risk: cold nodule on scintigraphy is more concerning; papillary carcinoma most common thyroid cancer
Initiate levothyroxine when: overt hypothyroidism (elevated TSH + low fT4), subclinical with TSH >10, pregnancy (start immediately even if subclinical), symptoms + TSH 5–10 + positive anti-TPO

Hyperthyroidism treatment decision: refer to endocrinology for Graves (radioactive iodine, antithyroid drug, thyroidectomy options); NP may start beta-blocker for symptom control while awaiting endocrinology
Referral to endocrinology: pregnancy + thyroid disease, refractory to initial therapy, nodule with suspicious features, pediatric thyroid disease, secondary hypothyroidism
Treatment goal: target TSH within normal range (0.5–4.5 mIU/L) for most adults; suppress to 0.1–0.5 for thyroid cancer post-thyroidectomy

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: SF-02 slugA enforced: questions cover TSH interpretation, Graves vs Hashimoto distinction, subclinical treatment thresholds, and referral decisions. May NOT include levothyroxine starting dose, weight-based dosing, administration timing, drug interactions, or titration — those belong to levothyroxine-dosing-monitoring-interactions (SF-02 slugB). No overlap with Phase 1 thyroid recognition questions (np-p1-051, np-p1-052 covered symptom pattern recognition only — 2A extends to laboratory classification and treatment initiation decision).

lessons.title: Thyroid Disease: Levothyroxine Dosing, Titration, and Monitoring
learningObjectives: Calculate levothyroxine starting dose using weight-based formula (1.6 mcg/kg/day) with elderly/cardiac modifications
Apply TSH monitoring interval after levothyroxine initiation or dose change (6–8 weeks)
Identify absorption interactions that require timing separation: calcium, iron, PPIs, sucralfate, cholestyramine
Recognize drug interactions that alter levothyroxine requirements: rifampin, phenytoin, carbamazepine
Apply dose titration increments (12.5–25 mcg steps) and target TSH by age group

keyPoints: Full replacement dose: 1.6 mcg/kg/day (ideal body weight) for otherwise healthy adults with primary hypothyroidism
Elderly or cardiac patients: start low, go slow — 25–50 mcg daily; titrate gradually over months to avoid precipitating angina or arrhythmia from increased metabolic demand
Titration: increase in 12.5–25 mcg increments; recheck TSH 6–8 weeks after EACH dose change (T4 half-life ≈7 days; steady-state requires ≥4–6 weeks)
Pregnancy: thyroid requirements increase ≈30–50% in the first trimester; increase dose by 30% immediately on confirmed pregnancy in known hypothyroid patient
Levothyroxine is best absorbed on an empty stomach (30–60 min before breakfast) — food, especially high-fiber, reduces absorption

Medications that REDUCE levothyroxine absorption (must be taken 4 hours apart): calcium carbonate, ferrous sulfate, PPIs (reduce gastric acid needed for some absorption), sucralfate, cholestyramine, colestipol
Drug interactions that INCREASE levothyroxine metabolism (require dose increase): rifampin (strong CYP induction), phenytoin, carbamazepine — these accelerate T4 clearance
Brand vs generic: switching between brand (Synthroid) and generic or between generic manufacturers requires TSH recheck — bioequivalence differs slightly between manufacturers
After initiation: TSH at 6–8 weeks; adjust dose; repeat at 6–8 weeks after each adjustment
Stable patient: annual TSH check
Pregnancy: TSH every 4 weeks in first trimester, then every trimester; adjust dose immediately to keep TSH <2.5 mIU/L in first trimester

Overtreatment signs (TSH suppressed below normal): atrial fibrillation risk, osteoporosis (especially postmenopausal women), anxiety — do not suppress TSH below normal range except in thyroid cancer management

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: SF-02 slugB enforced: this lesson exclusively covers dosing, titration, absorption, and monitoring. May NOT include TSH interpretation for diagnosis, Graves vs Hashimoto distinction, treatment initiation decision — those belong to thyroid-disease-diagnosis-management (SF-02 slugA). All questions must anchor on an existing patient already on levothyroxine (follow-up or adjustment scenario) or on absorption/interaction clinical decision.

lessons.title: CKD Management: Slowing Progression and Monitoring
learningObjectives: Apply KDIGO CKD staging by eGFR (G1–G5) and albuminuria (A1–A3) to determine monitoring frequency and prognosis
Select renoprotective agents: RAAS inhibitors for albuminuria reduction, SGLT-2 inhibitors for CKD progression
Apply blood pressure target in CKD with proteinuria (<130/80) and the preferred antihypertensive class
Identify dietary restrictions for CKD progression: sodium, potassium, phosphate, and protein thresholds
Determine referral criteria to nephrology: eGFR <30, rapidly declining eGFR, refractory hyperkalemia

keyPoints: CKD staging: G1 (eGFR ≥90 + structural/lab abnormality), G2 (60–89), G3a (45–59), G3b (30–44), G4 (15–29), G5 (<15 or dialysis)
Albuminuria staging: A1 (<30 mg/g), A2 (30–299 mg/g = microalbuminuria), A3 (≥300 mg/g = macroalbuminuria/proteinuria)
Monitoring frequency by risk: low risk (G1–G2/A1) = annual; moderate (G3a/A1 or G1/A2) = biannual; high/very high risk = every 3–4 months
Labs at each CKD visit: eGFR, serum potassium, bicarbonate, albumin; annually: PTH, phosphate, calcium, 25-OH Vitamin D, CBC (anemia of CKD)
RAAS inhibition (ACEi or ARB): first-line for CKD with ANY degree of albuminuria (A2 or A3); reduces intraglomerular pressure and proteinuria; do NOT combine ACEi + ARB

SGLT-2 inhibitors (canagliflozin, dapagliflozin, empagliflozin): proven to slow CKD progression independently of diabetes (CREDENCE, DAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY trials) — now recommended for CKD with UACR ≥200 mg/g or eGFR 20–45
BP control: target <130/80 in CKD with proteinuria — most important modifiable CKD progression factor after RAAS blockade
Smoking cessation: doubles rate of CKD progression in smokers — NP must counsel and facilitate cessation
Sodium: <2g/day (reduces proteinuria and BP) — most important dietary modification in CKD
Potassium: restrict in G3b–G5 or if hyperkalemia (K⁺ >5.0); avoid high-potassium foods (bananas, oranges, tomatoes, potatoes) when K⁺ elevated
Protein: modest restriction 0.6–0.8 g/kg/day in G3–G5 not on dialysis (higher protein accelerates progression)
Phosphate: restrict in G4–G5 (hyperphosphatemia drives secondary hyperparathyroidism and vascular calcification)

Nephrology referral criteria: eGFR <30 (G4–G5), rapidly declining eGFR (>5 mL/min/year loss), refractory hyperkalemia, nephrotic-range proteinuria, unexplained hematuria with proteinuria, suspected GN

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: SF-03 slugA enforced: May NOT include Cockcroft-Gault formula, specific drug-to-avoid lists (metformin, NSAIDs, nitrofurantoin below eGFR thresholds), antibiotic dose adjustments, or DOAC dosing — those belong to medication-dose-adjustment-ckd (SF-03 slugB). No overlap with Phase 1 CKD anemia question (np-p1-058 covered EPO deficiency recognition only).

lessons.title: CKD: Complication Management — Anemia, Acidosis, MBD, and Medication Safety
learningObjectives: Recognize and manage anemia of CKD: EPO deficiency mechanism, iron status assessment, ESA initiation threshold
Apply metabolic acidosis management in CKD: sodium bicarbonate threshold and benefit
Identify CKD-mineral bone disease (CKD-MBD): PTH, phosphate, calcium, and Vitamin D monitoring and treatment sequence
Manage secondary hyperparathyroidism: calcitriol vs active Vitamin D analogs and phosphate binders
Apply medication safety rules in CKD at the SF-03 slugA boundary: RAAS monitoring, MRA caution, hyperkalemia management

keyPoints: Mechanism: EPO deficiency (decreased renal EPO production); also iron deficiency (often concurrent), shortened RBC survival
Workup: CBC + reticulocyte count + serum iron/TIBC/ferritin + transferrin saturation (TSAT); exclude other causes if Hgb <10 g/dL in G3
Iron optimization before ESA: TSAT <20% or ferritin <200 ng/mL → give iron first (oral or IV); IV iron preferred in G4–G5 (GI intolerance + poor absorption with oral)
ESA initiation threshold: Hgb <10 g/dL after iron optimization; target Hgb 10–12 g/dL (do NOT target Hgb >13 — increased CV events in CHOIR/TREAT trials)
Darbepoetin or epoetin alfa dosing belongs to nephrology/managed protocol — NP role: recognize indication, initiate nephrology co-management
Metabolic acidosis in CKD (G3b+): bicarbonate <22 mEq/L → start sodium bicarbonate supplementation; target serum bicarbonate ≥22 mEq/L — reduces CKD progression rate and protein catabolism
CKD-MBD sequence: declining eGFR → phosphate retention → hyperphosphatemia → reduced 1,25 Vit D → hypocalcemia → elevated PTH (secondary hyperparathyroidism)
Monitoring: check PTH, phosphate, calcium at G3 annually; more frequently at G4–G5
Phosphate management: dietary restriction + phosphate binders (calcium carbonate, sevelamer, lanthanum — do NOT use aluminum-based in non-dialysis CKD)
Vitamin D: Vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol) for simple deficiency; calcitriol (active 1,25 Vit D) for secondary hyperparathyroidism in G4–G5

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: SF-03 slugA boundary applies: 2B covers CKD-specific complication management at the monitoring/initiation threshold level. Drug dose adjustment tables (antibiotic dosing, DOAC adjustments) belong to medication-dose-adjustment-ckd (SF-03 slugB) and are excluded from 2B.

lessons.title: COPD: GOLD Staging and Pharmacological Management
learningObjectives: Apply GOLD 2023 staging by spirometry (FEV1/FVC <0.7 + severity) and symptom burden (mMRC, CAT score)
Select inhaler therapy by GOLD group (A, B, E): SABA, LAMA, LABA, ICS combinations
Distinguish SABA (rescue) from LAMA/LABA (maintenance) mechanism and appropriate use
Identify ICS indication in COPD: frequent exacerbations or eosinophilia — and risk of ICS in COPD (pneumonia)
Apply smoking cessation as the most effective intervention to slow COPD progression

keyPoints: Spirometry requirement: FEV1/FVC <0.7 post-bronchodilator is required for diagnosis; GOLD 1 (FEV1 ≥80%), GOLD 2 (50–79%), GOLD 3 (30–49%), GOLD 4 (<30%)
Symptom burden: mMRC dyspnea scale (0–4) and CAT score (0–40); CAT ≥10 or mMRC ≥2 = significant symptoms
GOLD groups: Group A (low risk, low symptoms), Group B (low risk, high symptoms), Group E (high risk with ≥2 exacerbations/year or ≥1 hospitalization)
Exacerbation history is the key distinction between Group B and E — NOT spirometry severity alone
Group A: offer bronchodilator (SABA or SAMA PRN) — no maintenance therapy required if symptoms are minimal and no exacerbations
Group B: LAMA or LABA (long-acting bronchodilator monotherapy); combined LAMA+LABA for persistent symptoms (IMPACT trial supports dual bronchodilation)
Group E: LAMA + LABA as baseline; add ICS (triple therapy) if eosinophil count ≥300 cells/μL OR ≥2 moderate exacerbations/year despite dual bronchodilation
ICS caution: increases pneumonia risk in COPD (unlike asthma) — do NOT use ICS as monotherapy in COPD; only as part of triple therapy when indicated
Roflumilast (PDE4 inhibitor): add-on for GOLD Group E with chronic bronchitis (productive cough) and frequent exacerbations; used in non-smoking patients with FEV1 <50%
Smoking cessation: single most effective intervention — reduces FEV1 decline from ≈50 mL/year (smoker) to ≈25 mL/year (non-smoker normal aging rate)
Pulmonary rehabilitation: improves exercise tolerance and quality of life; indicated in Group B and E with persistent dyspnea
Vaccinations: annual influenza, COVID-19, and pneumococcal vaccines (PCV20 or PCV15 + PPSV23) in all COPD patients
Supplemental oxygen: indicated if resting PaO2 ≤55 mmHg or SpO2 ≤88% — reduces pulmonary hypertension and improves survival

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: 2A owns GOLD staging, inhaler class selection, and ICS indication. Questions must specify GOLD group or exacerbation history to anchor the therapeutic decision. Do not test COPD exacerbation treatment (antibiotics, steroids) — that belongs to Phase 3 or acute care domain. No overlap with Phase 1 dyspnea-differential-cardiac-pulmonary COPD recognition content (which covered diagnosis and spirometry interpretation at the differential level).

lessons.title: COPD Exacerbation Recognition, Management, and Hospitalization Criteria
learningObjectives: Classify COPD exacerbation severity: mild (outpatient), moderate (oral steroids + antibiotics outpatient), severe (hospitalization)
Apply antibiotic selection for COPD exacerbation with purulent sputum: first-line agents
Identify hospitalization criteria for COPD exacerbation: DECAF score, hypercapnia, altered mental status
Recognize post-exacerbation management: oral corticosteroid taper duration, reassessment, inhaler escalation
Identify complications of COPD: cor pulmonale, polycythemia, spontaneous pneumothorax risk

keyPoints: Mild exacerbation: increased dyspnea, sputum, but manageable at home with bronchodilator increase; no systemic symptoms

Moderate exacerbation (most common NP decision): increased dyspnea + purulent sputum + ≥2 of 3 Anthonisen criteria (dyspnea, sputum volume, sputum purulence) → oral prednisone 40 mg/day × 5 days + antibiotic
Antibiotic selection: amoxicillin-clavulanate or doxycycline (uncomplicated); azithromycin or levofloxacin (if high risk: ≥2 exac/year, FEV1 <50%, prior Pseudomonas, comorbidities)
Oral corticosteroid course: 5 days (not prolonged taper) — REDUCE trial: 5-day course equivalent to 14-day course
Hospital admission: severe dyspnea despite initial bronchodilators, SpO2 <90%, altered mental status, failure to improve at home, inadequate social support, hypercapnia (pCO2 >45 with acidosis)
NIV (BiPAP) indication: acute hypercapnic respiratory failure (pH 7.25–7.35, pCO2 >45) — reduces intubation and mortality
Post-exacerbation follow-up: within 30 days of discharge (high readmission risk); reassess inhaler technique, escalate therapy if ≥2 exacerbations/year, pulmonary rehabilitation referral
Cor pulmonale: RV hypertrophy from chronic hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction — presents as peripheral edema + elevated JVP + RV heave; supplemental oxygen is the primary treatment

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: 2B covers exacerbation management and hospitalization decision. Stems must specify severity anchor (purulent sputum, SpO2, prior exacerbation count). Antibiotic selection questions must specify risk stratification. No duplication with Phase 1 dyspnea differentiation content.

lessons.title: Hyperlipidemia: ASCVD Risk Stratification and Statin Selection
learningObjectives: Apply ACC/AHA pooled cohort equation to calculate 10-year ASCVD risk and select statin intensity
Distinguish statin intensity: low (rosuvastatin 5 mg, atorvastatin 10 mg), moderate, high (atorvastatin 40–80 mg, rosuvastatin 20–40 mg)
Identify four primary statin benefit groups per 2018 ACC/AHA guidelines
Manage statin intolerance: myopathy recognition, CK monitoring, switching or dose reduction
Apply add-on lipid therapy: ezetimibe (first add-on), PCSK9 inhibitors (refractory high-risk)

keyPoints: Four primary statin benefit groups: (1) clinical ASCVD — high-intensity statin; (2) T2DM age 40–75 — moderate-intensity statin; (3) LDL ≥190 mg/dL — high-intensity statin; (4) primary prevention 40–75 with 10-year ASCVD risk ≥7.5% — moderate-intensity statin (consider high-intensity if ≥20%)
High-intensity statins: atorvastatin 40–80 mg/day, rosuvastatin 20–40 mg/day (reduces LDL ≥50%)
Moderate-intensity: atorvastatin 10–20 mg, rosuvastatin 5–10 mg, simvastatin 20–40 mg (reduces LDL 30–50%)
LDL targets: ASCVD → LDL <70 mg/dL; very high risk ASCVD → LDL <55 mg/dL; T2DM + statin → LDL <70 mg/dL
Myopathy spectrum: myalgia (muscle pain, CK normal — very common, often not statin-related), myositis (muscle pain + CK >10× ULN), rhabdomyolysis (CK >10,000 + myoglobinuria + AKI — rare)
Statin intolerance management: switch to alternate statin (rosuvastatin every other day, pitavastatin), lower dose, or add ezetimibe to lower required statin dose

Drug interactions elevating statin levels (rhabdomyolysis risk): CYP3A4 inhibitors with simvastatin/lovastatin — avoid simvastatin with amiodarone, verapamil, diltiazem; switch to rosuvastatin or pravastatin (not CYP3A4 metabolized)
Ezetimibe: blocks intestinal cholesterol absorption; reduces LDL additional 15–20%; add when statin alone insufficient (IMPROVE-IT trial: ezetimibe + statin reduced CV events post-ACS)
PCSK9 inhibitors (evolocumab, alirocumab): injectable, high cost; for very high-risk ASCVD with LDL still ≥70 mg/dL on max tolerated statin + ezetimibe
Pregnancy: all statins are CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy (teratogenic — category X); discontinue when pregnancy is confirmed or planning
Elderly (>75): moderate-intensity statin preferred; high-intensity may have more adverse effects without additional benefit in primary prevention

Hypertriglyceridemia: fasting TG >500 mg/dL → treat to prevent pancreatitis; omega-3 fatty acids (icosapent ethyl/Vascepa — REDUCE-IT trial: reduced CV events in statin-treated T2DM/ASCVD patients with TG 135–499)
Familial hypercholesterolemia: LDL ≥190 mg/dL at any age → high-intensity statin regardless of 10-year risk score; refer to preventive cardiologist if not at goal

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: Lipid questions must specify the 10-year ASCVD risk, comorbidity context, or current statin intensity when testing escalation or safety decisions. Statin-drug interaction questions must name the interacting drug and require the learner to identify the unsafe combination. No overlap with afib anticoagulation (which uses statins as a secondary question topic in some cases).

lessons.title: Anemia Workup and Treatment: Iron-Deficiency, B12/Folate, and Hemolytic Causes
learningObjectives: Apply a CBC-based framework to classify anemia: microcytic, normocytic, macrocytic — and generate targeted differentials for each
Select iron replacement route and dose for IDA based on severity and GI tolerability
Distinguish B12 from folate deficiency by subacute combined degeneration risk (B12 only)
Manage hemolytic anemia: distinguish intrinsic (G6PD, hereditary spherocytosis, sickle cell) from extrinsic (AIHA, TTP/HUS, microangiopathic)
Identify indications for transfusion vs oral supplementation in anemia management

keyPoints: Microcytic (MCV <80): IDA (low ferritin, low serum iron, high TIBC), thalassemia (normal-low ferritin, target cells, elevated RBC count), anemia of chronic disease (ACD — ferritin elevated, low serum iron, low TIBC)
Normocytic (MCV 80–100): ACD (most common normocytic anemia), hemolytic anemias, aplastic anemia, renal anemia, early IDA or B12 deficiency
Macrocytic (MCV >100): B12 deficiency, folate deficiency, alcohol use (direct marrow toxicity), hypothyroidism, medications (methotrexate, hydroxyurea, azathioprine, trimethoprim)
Reticulocyte count: low → poor production (IDA, B12, aplastic); elevated → peripheral destruction or blood loss (hemolysis, acute hemorrhage)
Diagnosis: low ferritin (<12 ng/mL is diagnostic; <30 ng/mL is low-normal and should prompt investigation); low serum iron; elevated TIBC; microcytic hypochromic anemia on smear

Oral iron: ferrous sulfate 325 mg (65 mg elemental iron) TID between meals for maximal absorption; GI side effects (constipation, nausea) dose-limiting; take with Vitamin C (orange juice) to enhance absorption
Alternate-day dosing (Moretti trial): ferrous sulfate every other day achieves similar or better absorption as daily with fewer GI side effects — due to hepcidin suppression pattern
IV iron indications: GI intolerance to oral iron, malabsorption (celiac, post-bariatric), ongoing blood loss faster than oral can replete, CKD, inflammatory bowel disease
Response monitoring: reticulocytosis in 7–10 days; Hgb rise 1–2 g/dL in 4 weeks; continue 3 months after Hgb normalizes to replete stores

B12 deficiency: macrocytic anemia + subacute combined degeneration (posterior and lateral column — paresthesias, ataxia, Lhermitte sign); pernicious anemia (anti-intrinsic factor antibodies), strict vegan diet, gastric bypass; B12 IM injection (if absorption impaired) or high-dose oral 1000 mcg/day (adequate even in pernicious anemia if high enough dose)

Folate deficiency: macrocytic WITHOUT neurological complications; alcoholism, pregnancy, methotrexate, phenytoin; treat with folic acid 1 mg/day; ALWAYS rule out B12 deficiency first before folate (folate treats anemia but unmasks/accelerates B12 neuropathy)

G6PD deficiency: X-linked; hemolytic crisis triggered by oxidative stress (infections, primaquine, dapsone, nitrofurantoin, fava beans); Heinz bodies on smear; manage trigger avoidance + supportive care

AIHA (warm-type): positive direct Coombs test; associated with lupus, CLL, drugs; treat with prednisone; TTP: MAHA (schistocytes on smear) + thrombocytopenia + fever + AKI + neuro changes (pentad) → plasma exchange emergency

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: Anemia questions must specify the CBC indices (MCV, reticulocyte count, ferritin or key lab) in the stem — the clinical vignette must provide the necessary data to classify the anemia. TTP recognition question must include pentad features. No overlap with Phase 1 fatigue-differential anemia recognition questions (np-p1-053, np-p1-054 covered initial recognition and basic lab patterns — Phase 2 questions cover treatment decisions and advanced workup).
